title: Platform Guidelines
source: Starter KB compiled from primary platform guidance: Apple Human Interface Guidelines (iOS/iPadOS/macOS), Material Design (Android/Web), WAI-ARIA Authoring Practices (web interaction patterns), and MDN/WHATWG HTML guidance (web form behaviors like autocomplete).
purpose: Provide reference knowledge for evaluating and aligning UI designs with platform conventions across iOS, Android (Material), and the web. Helps ensure predictable navigation, appropriate component choices, consistent behaviors, and accessible interaction patterns.
scope: Applies to user-facing UI design for iOS/iPadOS, Android/Material, and web apps: navigation patterns, common components, interaction behaviors, states, and web semantic expectations. Excludes assistant behavior, output formatting, schema enforcement, and design-system-specific rules (handled by Design System KBs).
definitions: A widely expected UI behavior or pattern on a platform (e.g., iOS tab bar for top-level destinations; Material navigation bar/bottom navigation for top-level destinations).
Navigation that switches between primary areas of an app (e.g., tabs / bottom nav).
Navigation within a section (e.g., push to detail, back to list).
A temporary overlay that requires the user to complete or dismiss it before returning to the underlying context (commonly used for focused tasks).
Using native HTML elements and/or correct ARIA roles/states so assistive technologies can interpret controls and patterns (e.g., combobox behavior).
rulesConstraints: Prefer platform-native patterns for navigation and core controls. Deviations must have a clear user benefit and be applied consistently.
Use top-level navigation for switching primary destinations; use hierarchical navigation for drilling in/out within a destination.
Avoid mixing multiple top-level nav paradigms on the same surface (e.g., tab bar + bottom nav) unless there is a strong, well-tested rationale.
Match component choice to platform expectations: use platform-standard controls for switches, selection, dialogs, and navigation where possible.
Make interactive states clear and consistent (enabled, disabled, pressed, focused).
Accessibility is not optional: ensure patterns are operable with assistive tech. On web, prefer native HTML controls; use WAI-ARIA APG patterns when building custom widgets.
Web forms should support autofill where appropriate; use the standard `autocomplete` tokens and do not invent custom values.
When designing cross-platform experiences, standardize the underlying IA and tasks, but allow platform-specific UI conventions (e.g., placement of navigation controls) to differ.

do: Use iOS tab bars for switching between top-level sections and keep destinations stable and recognizable.
Use Material navigation bars/bottom navigation for 3–5 primary destinations; keep labels clear and consistent.
Use top app bars for screen-level navigation and actions; keep key actions visible and avoid overcrowding.
Use dialogs for high-stakes confirmations and complex decisions; prefer non-blocking feedback (snackbars/toasts) for low-risk confirmations and undo.
On web, use native inputs/buttons/links when possible; for custom widgets, follow WAI-ARIA APG interaction models (keyboard, roles, states).
For web form fields, specify `autocomplete` where it improves entry speed and accuracy (e.g., name, email, address).
Design for back/escape behavior: users should be able to leave or undo safely (especially for modals and destructive actions).
dont: Don't reinvent common platform controls (switches, pickers, navigation) without a strong reason; it increases learning cost and accessibility risk.
Don't hide primary navigation behind ambiguous gestures or icon-only controls without labels when clarity is needed.
Don't overuse modals; avoid blocking the user for low-risk confirmations or routine feedback.
Don't create custom web widgets without specifying keyboard behavior, focus management, and ARIA semantics that match an established pattern.
Don't disable or sabotage web autofill by using nonstandard `autocomplete` values or unusual field structures unless required.
Don't rely on hover-only interactions for core actions in touch environments.

examples: Use a tab bar to switch between primary areas (e.g., Home, Search, Profile). Keep the same destinations available throughout typical navigation paths.
### Material: Top app bar + navigation bar
Use a top app bar for contextual title/actions and a bottom navigation bar for 3–5 primary destinations; avoid placing too many competing actions in the app bar.
If users must pick from a large list with type-ahead, use a combobox pattern with correct keyboard support; for a small fixed set, use a native select or radio group.
For a shipping address form, include standard `autocomplete` tokens (e.g., `shipping street-address`, `postal-code`) to support browser autofill.
edgeCases: Cross-platform parity: keep information architecture consistent, but allow platform-specific UI differences (e.g., iOS uses tab bars and back navigation conventions; Material uses navigation bar/drawer patterns).
Dense enterprise UIs: prioritize scannability and predictable navigation; avoid hidden actions that require discovery (especially icon-only controls without labels).
Custom components: when not using native controls, explicitly define keyboard interaction, focus order, and accessible roles/states; reference ARIA APG patterns for web.
Hybrid apps (web in mobile shells): be careful mixing web patterns with native expectations; validate back behavior, modal dismissal, and focus/keyboard interactions.
Internationalization: ensure labels fit longer strings; avoid hard-coded truncation that hides meaning in navigation destinations and key actions.
